did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses DAB-DETR, a deep learning-based object detection model (DETR with Dynamic Anchor Boxes), to detect and localize melanoma skin lesions in dermoscopic images. The study involves training and evaluating this AI model on a curated dataset of 650 annotated dermoscopic images to obtain results on lesion detection performance (AP, Recall) compared to other models like Deformable DETR and Faster R-CNN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors trained and evaluated a novel object detection model (DAB-DETR) on a curated dataset of 650 dermoscopic images. It reports specific performance metrics (AP, Recall) and compares the proposed method against baseline models (Deformable DETR, Faster R-CNN, etc.), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Object Detection on Detecting Skin Lesion using Dab-DETR
journal: 2025 15th International Conference on Computer and Knowledge Engineering (ICCKE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of detecting small and irregular melanoma skin lesions in dermoscopic images by proposing DAB-DETR, a DETR-based model incorporating dynamic anchor boxes. The authors trained and evaluated the model on a curated dataset of 650 annotated dermoscopic images covering four melanoma-related classes. Performance was assessed using standard object detection metrics, including Average Precision (AP) and Recall, and compared against baselines such as Deformable DETR, RetinaNet, and Faster R-CNN. The results demonstrated that DAB-DETR achieved an AP of 54.2%, outperforming Deformable DETR's 51.8%, with improved AP50 and AP75 scores. Additionally, the model showed increased recall from 73.7% to 75.0%, indicating better sensitivity to hard-to-detect cases. These findings suggest that DAB-DETR effectively improves lesion localization and convergence, offering a more accurate tool for automated melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting melanoma skin cancer. The curated dataset of 650 dermoscopic images encompasses four specific melanoma-related classes: Cancer, Acral Lentiginous, Lentigo Maligna, and Nodular Melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates and class labels for melanoma skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on object detection to localize and classify melanoma lesions in dermoscopic images. The model (DAB-DETR) predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities for four melanoma-related classes (Cancer, Acral Lentiginous, Lentigo Maligna, Nodular Melanoma). Performance is evaluated using object detection metrics: Average Precision (AP), AP50, AP75, and Recall.

datasets_used.name: Curated Dermoscopic Dataset (via Roboflow)
datasets_used.description: A dataset specifically curated for object detection tasks involving melanoma skin cancer, provided via Roboflow. It consists of dermoscopic images annotated in YOLO and COCO JSON formats.

use.training: Used to train the DAB-DETR model for 100 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance (AP, Recall) against baselines.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between DAB-DETR and baseline models were based on absolute metric values (AP, Recall) without statistical significance testing.

Curated Dermoscopic Dataset (via Roboflo.notes: DAB-DETR outperformed all baseline models. It showed a clear improvement over Deformable DETR (AP 54.2% vs 51.8%). It also outperformed Faster R-CNN variants, RetinaNet, and Standard DETR. The model demonstrated improved sensitivity (Recall 75.0%) and better localization accuracy at stricter IoU thresholds (AP75 55.1%).

architecture_used.main_model: DAB-DETR (DEtection TRansformer with Dynamic Anchor Boxes)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the paper mentions that DINO (a related model) uses denoising training and mixed query initialization, but does not specify pretraining details for the specific DAB-DETR implementation used in this study.

architecture_details.query_mechanism: Dynamic anchor boxes (bounding box coordinates) used as queries, updated layer-by-layer during decoding
architecture_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy classification loss and bounding box loss (L1 distance)

classification_or_output_head.output: Class probabilities and bounding box coordinates

conclusions: DAB-DETR effectively addresses key limitations of existing DETR-based methods, specifically slow convergence and difficulties in detecting small or irregularly shaped lesions common in dermoscopic imaging.
The proposed DAB-DETR model outperformed baseline models including Deformable DETR, Standard DETR, RetinaNet, and Faster R-CNN variants, achieving an Average Precision (AP) of 54.2%.
DAB-DETR demonstrated improved sensitivity to hard-to-detect cases, with Recall increasing from 73.7% (Deformable DETR) to 75.0%.

The model showed superior performance at stricter Intersection-over-Union (IoU) thresholds (AP50: 61.3%, AP75: 55.1%), indicating accurate lesion boundary localization critical for clinical diagnosis.

The use of dynamic anchor boxes as queries, updated layer-by-layer, provides explicit spatial priors that enhance query-to-feature alignment and mitigate the slow convergence problem inherent in standard DETR architectures.
DAB-DETR is a more practical and accurate tool for automated melanoma detection in clinical settings compared to traditional object detection models and standard transformer-based detectors.
Future work is needed to incorporate multi-modal data, optimize for real-time deployment, and explore advanced training techniques to further improve generalization and clinical applicability.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, curated dataset of only 650 dermoscopic images (455 training, 130 validation, 65 testing), which limits the generalizability and statistical robustness of the findings.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the internal test split of the curated dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and clinical applicability.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between DAB-DETR and baseline models.

The dataset is described as 'curated' via Roboflow with specific classes (Cancer, Acral Lentiginous, Lentigo Maligna, Nodular Melanoma), but details regarding demographic diversity, skin tone distribution, or potential selection bias are not provided.
Performance metrics (AP 54.2%) are modest, indicating that while DAB-DETR outperforms baselines, there is still significant room for improvement in detecting melanoma lesions.
The hardware used for training (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060, 6 GB VRAM) is consumer-grade, and computational efficiency or inference speed (critical for clinical deployment) is not reported.
The paper acknowledges future work is needed for real-time deployment and multi-modal data integration, suggesting current limitations in practical clinical workflow integration.
